title: Audit Scheme files for unguarded load-shared-object calls that crash static binaries
description: Scan .ss/.sls files and report any top-level load-shared-object or foreign-procedure calls that are not guarded by a JEMACS_STATIC (or similar) env-var check. Unguarded calls crash static binaries at boot time. Should report: file, line, the unguarded call, and whether a guard pattern exists nearby.
use_case: When preparing a Jerboa project for static binary compilation (musl or otherwise), before spending 30+ minutes on a Docker build that fails at runtime.
example_scenario: Building jemacs-qt static: (chez-qt ffi) calls load-shared-object at top level. Without JEMACS_STATIC=1 the compilation step fails with a dlopen error inside the container. Finding this required reading vendor/chez-qt/chez-qt/ffi.ss manually. A tool scan would have flagged it immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per static build session (eliminates 3-5 grep/read cycles to find unguarded FFI calls)

title: Verify Jerboa code blocks inside markdown docs compile and run
description: Scan a .md file (or directory) for fenced ```scheme / ```jerboa code blocks, extract each one, run it through the compiler, and optionally evaluate it. Report per-block status: compiled/failed/ran/threw, with line number of the fence and the exact error. Support an `:imports` metadata line before the fence (or a doc-level header) so the tool knows which modules each block expects. For blocks that are partial (no imports, no top-level wrapping), auto-wrap in `(import (jerboa prelude)) ...`. Intended for keeping design docs, cookbook sources, and README examples in lockstep with reality.
use_case: When shipping a design doc or porting guide that contains many worked code examples, before committing. Also for periodic audits of existing docs to catch drift when APIs change. Would have prevented shipping docs/core-async.md with two broken references (`@out` reader sugar that doesn't exist, `(swap! out cons v)` with wrong argument order) — both only discovered by running the example manually after commit.
example_scenario: docs/core-async.md Section 17 contains a 20-line worked example that ports Clojure's scatter/gather pattern to Jerboa. Two lines use `@out` (Clojure atom-deref reader sugar that doesn't exist in Jerboa) and one uses `(swap! out cons v)` instead of `(swap! out (cut cons v <>))`. A `jerboa_doc_verify docs/core-async.md` call would have compiled the block, noted "variable @out is not bound" at the fence's line, and caught both errors in one shot. Instead I wrote a /tmp/test-async-example.ss file, ran it by hand, saw the first error, fixed it, re-ran, saw the second, fixed it, re-ran again. Three iterations that one tool call could collapse into one. Same friction applies to every cookbook recipe on disk and every README code snippet.
estimated_token_reduction: ~1500 tokens per doc-ship session (replaces: write tmp file → read → run → parse error → re-write → re-run → parse → fix → re-run cycle). Multiplicative across every code block in a large doc.

title: Make safe APIs the default prelude exports
description: The (jerboa prelude) should re-export safe-sqlite-open as sqlite-open, safe-tcp-connect as tcp-connect, etc. Claude and humans will always reach for the simplest import — if the safe version IS the default, nobody has to remember to use (std safe). Raw APIs should require explicit (import (std db sqlite-native)) opt-in.
use_case: Every time Claude writes Jerboa code, it imports (jerboa prelude). If that gives it the safe APIs by default, all generated code is automatically type-checked and resource-safe without Claude knowing about the safety modules.
example_scenario: Claude writes (import (jerboa prelude)) then (sqlite-open "db") — this silently calls safe-sqlite-open which validates types, and (with-resource ...) is the documented pattern. No separate (import (std safe)) needed.
note: Requires Jerboa core change (prelude.sls), not implementable in jerboa-mcp.

title: jerboa_prompt_to_code: Generate verified Jerboa from natural language prompts
description: Tool that takes pseudocode/prompts, generates Jerboa via LLM, then verifies with eval/syntax_check/verify loop, iterating until passing. Outputs working .ss snippet.
use_case: AI assistants generating safe, executable Scheme from user descriptions without manual fixes.
example_scenario: User: "Write a function to sort a list and compute sum." Tool: Generates def/sort/sum code, verifies, returns verified snippet.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per code gen session (auto-verify reduces back-and-forth).
note: Requires calling an LLM API from within an MCP tool — not practical without Anthropic API integration.

title: jerboa_ai_scaffold: Generate ML/AI patterns from Rust crates
description: Scaffold Jerboa FFI wrappers for Rust ML libs (e.g., tch-rs tensors, ndarray stats); auto-generates safe bindings with type checks and examples.
use_case: Prototyping AI models in Jerboa without manual FFI boilerplate.
example_scenario: User: "Scaffold tensor ops from tch-rs." Tool: Generates foreign-procedure defs, safe wrappers, test suite.
note: Requires LLM API + knowledge of Rust crate APIs — too domain-specific without crate introspection tooling.

title: jerboa_semantic_search: Embeddings-based search over stdlib and cookbooks
description: Vector search for functions/patterns by intent (e.g., "safe file read"); uses simple embeddings on docstrings/exports for code discovery.
use_case: AI finding relevant APIs without exact name knowledge.
example_scenario: Query: "Handle JSON errors safely." Returns: json-parse with try/catch examples from cookbook.
note: Keyword-based version implemented as jerboa_stdlib_search. True semantic/embeddings search requires a vector store or embedding API.

title: Scanner to detect worker-thread mutation of shared hash tables and lists
description: Add a static analysis pass that flags the anti-pattern where a (spawn ...) worker thread's body (including parameterized callbacks) calls hash-put!, hash-remove!, set-car!, set-cdr!, or struct setters on values that are ALSO read by the thread that called spawn. This is unsafe under Chez preemptive pthreads — hash tables and pair lists are not thread-safe, and empirical testing shows 33–37% data loss on concurrent mutation even with per-op mutexes. The scanner should recommend the mailbox pattern (thread-send / non-blocking thread-receive 0 drain) as the fix.
use_case: Any Jerboa project using (spawn ...) with worker threads that drive a UI or long-running loop. The bug is invisible in development (messages appear to work) but manifests as silent data loss or hangs under load. A static pass that flags this pattern at lint time would save hours of debugging per occurrence.
example_scenario: jerboa-code TUI spawned a worker thread that called (hash-put! tool-counts name ...) and mutated msg-block lists via a parameterized callback. The main event-loop thread read these same structures to render each frame. Under load the TUI would hang randomly, losing ~1 in 3 events. Took Gemini 3.1 Pro pseudocode analysis to diagnose. A linter that recognized "callback defined inside parameterize inside spawn" + "mutates value reachable from outer scope" would have flagged this immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~3000 tokens per occurrence (avoids multi-round debugging session, .log analysis, thread-safety recipe search)

title: Batch symbol-existence check across prelude and (std ...) modules
description: A tool that takes an array of symbol names (function/macro/variable names) and returns, for each, whether it exists anywhere in `(jerboa prelude)` + all `(std ...)` modules, with its defining module, kind (proc/macro/syntax/var), and arity if known. Equivalent to running `jerboa_apropos` (or the existing function_signature tool) N times in parallel, but in a single call. Results would look like `[{name: "merge-with", exists: false}, {name: "select-keys", exists: true, module: "(std clojure)", kind: "proc", arity: "2"}, ...]`. Should default to an exact-name lookup, with an optional `fuzzy` flag to also include close matches.
use_case: When updating documentation that enumerates whether a set of external-ecosystem names (Clojure's, Racket's, R6RS's) are actually present in Jerboa. Also during porting work, when you need to quickly know which names from a source file will resolve vs need polyfills. Any time the question is "do these N functions exist in Jerboa?"
example_scenario: While updating `docs/clojure-vs-jerboa.md` to add status markers, I needed to verify whether Clojure names like `merge-with`, `zipmap`, `reduce-kv`, `memoize`, `iterate`, `repeatedly`, `fnil`, `every-pred`, `some-fn`, `trampoline`, `min-key`, `max-key`, `min-by`, `max-by`, `iterate-n` exist in Jerboa. I ran 5 separate `Grep` calls across `lib/` to check — a total of ~15 symbol lookups. A single `jerboa_symbol_exists_batch` call with the list would have returned `{select-keys: exists, merge: exists, everything-else: absent}` in one shot. Also discovered that the existing doc had several stale claims about non-existent functions (iterate-n, min-by, max-by, fnil-in-prelude, every-pred-in-prelude) because no one had cheap way to sanity-check a batch of names.
estimated_token_reduction: ~600 tokens per doc-verification pass (replaces 4-6 grep calls + result inspection with 1 structured tool call). Higher for porting workflows that cross-check dozens of names.

title: Detect fork-unsafe calls (run-safe-eval, subprocess) inside fiber handlers
description: Static analysis pass that flags calls to run-safe-eval, open-process-ports, or other fork()-using APIs when they appear inside HTTP handler functions, transducer lambdas (filtering/mapping bodies), or channel callbacks (go blocks, fiber thunks). These contexts are fibers — fork() from a fiber silently corrupts the scheduler. The tool should trace the call graph: if handler-fn calls eval-fn which calls run-safe-eval, flag the whole chain. Should report the fiber entry-point (e.g., route-get handler name) and the unsafe call site.
use_case: Any service using fiber-httpd or CSP channels that wants to use sandboxed eval for user-provided code. The fork-in-fiber bug produces zero error output — events simply stop processing. Diagnosing it requires knowing the fork/fiber interaction, which is non-obvious.
example_scenario: jerboa-edge Phase 2: eval-user-filter was initially implemented using run-safe-eval inside the transducer (which runs during fiber >!! calls). Filters appeared to register but never dropped events. No error logged anywhere. Root cause only found after ~15 tool calls investigating the filter logic, format strings, and alist key types — all red herrings. Switching to restricted-eval-string fixed it immediately. A static flag at authoring time would have prevented the entire debugging session.
estimated_token_reduction: ~3000 tokens per occurrence (eliminates multi-round silent-failure debugging: format-string investigation, alist-key investigation, eval environment investigation — all red herrings for a scheduler corruption bug)

title: Auto-run balance check after edits to functions with nesting depth > 4
description: When jerboa_balanced_replace, jerboa_splice_form, or jerboa_wrap_form edits a function whose body has nesting depth exceeding a threshold (e.g., 4 levels of let/cond/match nesting), automatically run jerboa_check_balance on the edited region and return any mismatch warnings inline with the edit result. This prevents the failure mode where a deep edit introduces a bracket mismatch that only surfaces as a parse error on the next cold restart — not during the current session where the already-running process masks it.
use_case: Any edit to deeply nested Scheme code — especially match+let loop+cond+let* combinations common in actor worker functions. The bug is invisible while the process is running (Chez Scheme re-reads on restart) and only surfaces as a cold-start parse error.
example_scenario: jerboa-edge make-worker had a real bracket mismatch (close sequence ))]) instead of ))]])) in a 5-level cond-inside-let*-inside-match-inside-let-loop. The balance checker flagged it each session but it was dismissed as a heuristic warning because the already-running service responded to health checks. On the next cold restart: Exception in read: bracketed list terminated by parenthesis. The entire Phase 2 filter testing session was on a service running from stale code — the fix hadn't taken effect at all.
estimated_token_reduction: ~500 tokens per deep edit (eliminates the restart→parse-error→debug cycle; inline warning at edit time is 1 line vs 3-5 tool calls to diagnose the cold-start failure)
